summary:
The Second Punic War (218–201 BC) was the most dramatic and existentially threatening of the [[event:1052|Punic Wars]], in which the Carthaginian general Hannibal Barca brought war directly into the Italian peninsula for fifteen years, inflicting a series of catastrophic defeats on Roman armies that tested the Republic's survival to its limits. No other foreign enemy came closer to ending Rome before it became an empire.

Hannibal's audacious [[event:1055|Hannibal's Crossing of the Alps]] with his army and war elephants opened the war with a strategic shock. He then defeated Roman armies in rapid succession: at the Trebia River (218 BC), [[event:1061|Battle of Lake Trasimene]] (217 BC), and most catastrophically at [[event:1056|Battle of Cannae]] (216 BC), where his double envelopment destroyed roughly 70,000 Romans in a single day — one of the worst defeats in Roman military history and a tactical model still studied in military academies. Roman Italy south of the Po Valley was his to march through; several Italian allies defected to Carthage.

Yet Rome did not collapse. The Senate refused to negotiate or ransom captives; it raised new armies from every available source. The Roman general Fabius Maximus pursued a strategy of harassment and attrition, avoiding pitched battle while cutting off Hannibal's foraging and supply — tactics that earned him the nickname Cunctator ('Delayer') and influenced military thinking down to the present. Gradually, Hannibal's position in Italy became untenable without Carthaginian reinforcements: the attempt to send his brother Hasdrubal with an army was ended when Hasdrubal was defeated and killed at the [[event:1062|Battle of the Metaurus]] in 207 BC.

The Roman counter-strategy under Publius Cornelius Scipio eventually took the war to Spain, where Hannibal's power base lay, and then to Africa. Scipio's invasion of Africa in 204 BC forced Carthage to recall Hannibal. At the [[event:1057|Battle of Zama]] in 202 BC, Scipio defeated Hannibal — the only defeat of Hannibal's career in a major battle. Carthage surrendered, paying an enormous indemnity, surrendering Spain and its navy, and accepting severe restrictions on its ability to wage war. Hannibal eventually fled to the east, dying in exile in Bithynia in 183 BC to avoid capture by Rome.
